paragraphs.en: Anna goes to the supermarket every Saturday morning. Today, she needs to buy food for the week. She takes a basket and walks inside the shop.
paragraphs.fr: Anna va au supermarche tous les samedis matin. Aujourd'hui, elle doit acheter de la nourriture pour la semaine. Elle prend un panier et entre dans le magasin.
Est-ce que je peux vous aider ?
paragraphs.speaker: Shop assistant

paragraphs.en: Yes, please. I need some bread and some milk. How much is the bread?
paragraphs.fr: Oui, s'il vous plait. J'ai besoin de pain et de lait. Combien coute le pain ?
paragraphs.speaker: Anna

paragraphs.en: The bread is one pound fifty. And the milk is ninety pence.
paragraphs.fr: Le pain coute une livre cinquante. Et le lait coute quatre-vingt-dix pence.
paragraphs.speaker: Shop assistant

paragraphs.en: Good, that's not expensive. I also need some apples. How much are the apples?
paragraphs.fr: Bien, ce n'est pas cher. J'ai aussi besoin de pommes. Combien coutent les pommes ?
paragraphs.speaker: Anna

paragraphs.en: The apples are two pounds a kilo. How many kilos do you want?
paragraphs.fr: Les pommes coutent deux livres le kilo. Combien de kilos voulez-vous ?
paragraphs.speaker: Shop assistant

paragraphs.en: One kilo, please. And six eggs. How much is that in total?
paragraphs.fr: Un kilo, s'il vous plait. Et six oeufs. Combien ca fait en tout ?
paragraphs.speaker: Anna

paragraphs.fr: Ca fait cinq livres quarante, s'il vous plait.
paragraphs.speaker: Shop assistant

paragraphs.en: Here you are. Thank you very much!
paragraphs.speaker: Anna

paragraphs.en: Anna puts everything in her basket. She is happy because the food is cheap today. She walks home with a big smile.
paragraphs.fr: Anna met tout dans son panier. Elle est contente parce que la nourriture n'est pas chere aujourd'hui. Elle rentre chez elle avec un grand sourire.

vocabulary.definition_en: un aliment courant fait a partir de farine et d'eau
vocabulary.example: I eat bread every morning for breakfast.

vocabulary.definition_en: un liquide blanc provenant des vaches que les gens boivent
vocabulary.example: I like milk in my tea.

vocabulary.definition_en: un fruit rond qui est rouge, vert ou jaune
vocabulary.example: I eat an apple every day after lunch.

vocabulary.definition_en: une unite de poids egale a 1 000 grammes
vocabulary.example: I need two kilos of oranges, please.

vocabulary.definition_en: un recipient utilise pour transporter des choses en faisant les courses
vocabulary.example: She puts the fruit in the basket.

vocabulary.definition_en: qui coute peu d'argent, pas cher
vocabulary.example: This shirt is very cheap — only five pounds!
That bag is too expensive. I can't buy it.

vocabulary.definition_en: obtenir quelque chose en payant de l'argent
vocabulary.example: I want to buy some oranges at the shop.

grammar_points.title: 'How much...?' vs. 'How many...?'
grammar_points.explanation: On utilise **'How much'** avec les choses que l'on ne peut pas compter (noms indenombrables comme bread, milk, water). On utilise **'How many'** avec les choses que l'on peut compter (noms denombrables comme apples, eggs, kilos). 'How much' s'utilise aussi pour demander le prix.
examples: How much is the bread? (= price)
How much milk do you need? (= uncountable)
How many apples do you want? (= countable)
How many kilos do you need? (= countable)

grammar_points.title: Countable vs. Uncountable Nouns
grammar_points.explanation: Les **noms denombrables** (countable nouns) ont une forme singuliere et plurielle : one apple, two apples. Les **noms indenombrables** (uncountable nouns) n'ont pas de forme plurielle : bread (PAS breads), milk (PAS milks). On utilise **'some'** avec les deux : 'some apples' (denombrable) et 'some bread' (indenombrable).
examples: Countable: an apple → three apples, an egg → six eggs
Uncountable: some bread, some milk, some water

expressions.example: How much is this bag? — It's ten pounds.
expressions.note: La facon la plus courante de demander le prix de quelque chose. Utilisez 'How much are they?' pour les articles au pluriel.

expressions.example: I need some milk and some eggs for the cake.
expressions.note: Utilisez 'some' dans les phrases affirmatives pour parler d'une quantite non precisee.

expressions.example: Twenty pounds for a sandwich? That's too expensive!
expressions.note: 'Too' signifie 'plus que ce qui est acceptable'. Il a un sens negatif.

expressions.example: Here you are — five pounds change.
expressions.note: Se dit quand on donne quelque chose a quelqu'un. Tres courant dans les magasins.

written.question: How much does Anna pay in total?
written.explanation: The shop assistant says 'That's five pounds forty, please.'

written.question: Complete: '________ eggs do you want?'
written.explanation: 'Eggs' is a countable noun, so we use 'How many'.

written.question: Which of these is an uncountable noun?
written.explanation: 'Bread' is uncountable. We say 'some bread', not 'a bread' or 'two breads'.

written.sentence: How ________ is the milk? — It's ninety pence.
written.hint: We ask about price with 'How much...?'
In positive sentences, we use 'some' with both countable and uncountable nouns.

written.french: Combien coutent les pommes ? — Elles coutent deux livres le kilo.
written.suggested_answer: How much are the apples? — They are two pounds a kilo.

oral.instruction: Read this dialogue aloud. Try to use different voices for Anna and the shop assistant.
oral.text:
Anna: I need some bread and some milk. How much is the bread?
Shop assistant: The bread is one pound fifty. And the milk is ninety pence.
Anna: Good, that's not expensive.
oral.focus: Practise the rising intonation on questions ('How much is the bread?') and falling intonation on statements.

oral.question: You are at the supermarket. Ask the price of three things and say if they are cheap or expensive.
oral.suggested_answer: How much is the bread? It's one pound. That's very cheap. How much are the oranges? They are three pounds a kilo. That's not too expensive. How much is the cheese? It's eight pounds. That's too expensive!
